segments.s377-g25: بشد is an archaic/past narrative marker meaning “it became / he went” (here: ‘he set out’). تیغ‌زن is a compound participial epithet “sword‑striking” or “sword‑wielder” placed before the name it qualifies. گردکش here functions as a name or epithet (not the literal noun ‘small round object’), and پور شاه is the patronymic ‘son of the king’ (پور = ‘son’, شاه = ‘king’), so the whole phrase strings an epithet + name + filiation in typical Persian noun‑phrase order.
segments.s377-g26: بگردید is a past/action verb meaning ‘he ranged/roamed/went about’ (here: ‘he swept across’). بر کشورش uses بر + noun to indicate motion over/through a domain (‘over his country’), where کشور + ش = ‘his realm’. با سپاه is an instrumental/companional phrase ‘with an army’ (با = with, سپاه = army).
segments.s377-g27: به ... برگذشت is a classical register verb phrase meaning ‘he went to / passed to’ (به = to/toward; برگذشت = ‘passed on/sent himself’ here = ‘proceeded to’). The two destinations are coordinated with و ‘and’: به روم و به هندوستان = ‘to Rome and to India’.
segments.s377-g28: ز ... گذشت is a simple past clause ‘he passed from / crossed …’. ز (poetic for از) = ‘from’, دریا = ‘the sea’, و تاریکی = ‘and darkness’, اندر = ‘into/through/in’, and گذشت = past ‘passed’. The phrase describes the manner/route: ‘he crossed the sea and went through darkness.’
segments.s377-g29: شه is a poetic/truncated form of شاه ‘king’ and here stands before a coordinated list of realms: روم، هندوستان، و یمن = ‘Rome, India, and Yemen’. This phrase names the rulers/kingdoms involved: ‘the kings of Rome and India and Yemen.’
segments.s377-g30: همه نام کردند بر تهمتن is an old construction meaning ‘all (they) made/appointed the name upon Tahmatan’ — i.e. ‘they all named him Tahmatan’. Here نام is the noun ‘name’ used as an object with کردند (‘they did/made’), and بر marks the person receiving the name (بر تهمتن = ‘on/unto Tahmatan’).
segments.s377-g31: This clause means ‘they asked him for a report about religion.’ وزو appears here as an archaic/poetic form equivalent to ‘از او’ (‘from him’), though the supplied lexical sense list gives ‘ablution’ and thus does not match; دین = ‘religion’, گزارش = ‘report/news’, همی is the continuous/narrative adverb often used with verbs (here giving a habitual/ongoing sense), and خواستند is the past ‘they asked/desired’. Grammatically: [from him] [religion‑report] [they continually asked].
segments.s377-g32: This clause reads ‘they made/arranged the religion right for Marin.’ مرین is treated here as a proper name (the recipient/affected person) in the supplied senses; دین = ‘religion’; به را is a fixed classical collocation meaning ‘properly / rightly’ (به = ‘to/for’, را = object/emphatic marker here tied into the phrase), and بیاراستند is the past ‘they adorned / they set in order’. The overall structure is subject‑verb with an indirect object (مرین) and a manner/adverbial (به را ‘properly’).
segments.s377-g73: سواران is the plural subject 'the riders/horsemen'. جهان را is the definite object marked by را (here marking what is being held: 'the world'). همی داشتند is an archaic past-continuous/habitual construction (همی + past plural داشتند) meaning 'were holding' or 'kept on holding' — همی gives iterative/continuous aspect and داشتند is the past form of داشتن used as the main verb 'held'.
segments.s377-g74: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'. برزیگران (pl.) is the agent 'the tillers/farmers'. تخم is the direct object 'seed'. می‌کاشتند is the classical imperfective past (mi‑ prefix + past stem + plural ending) giving past habitual/continuous 'they were sowing' or 'they used to sow'. The clause as a whole gives the time-frame for the preceding clause.
segments.s377-g75: بدین سان is an adverbial phrase 'in this way/thus' (بدین = 'in this' + سان = 'manner'), introducing the manner of what follows. ببوده is an archaic/perfective form meaning 'had been' (a past perfective or resultative), and سراسر جهان is an adverbial phrase 'throughout the world' that scopes over the following predicate.
segments.s377-g76: به گیتی here functions as a locative/idiomatic phrase 'in the world' modifying the predicate. شده is the perfect/passive/auxiliary participle 'has/had become' and is used with the following adjectives: گم 'lost/astray', بد 'bad/evil' and بدگمان 'suspicious/distrustful'. Classical Persian often stacks predicate adjectives after شده without conjunctions; the repetition (بد بدگمان) is poetic emphasis.
segments.s377-g65: فرسته (a messenger) is the direct object/topic placed before the verb in classical Persian; فرستاد is the 3rd-person singular past tense ‘sent/dispatched’. سوی پدر is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/to the father’ marking the recipient. The overall clause literally reads ‘(He) sent a messenger toward the father.’
segments.s377-g66: که‌ای is the archaic/formulaic vocative (often written as که ای) used to address someone directly; here it introduces direct speech. نامور and پیروزگر are epithets modifying شاه (name/title after the vocative): ‘renowned (نامور) king (شاه), triumphant (پیروزگر).’ Adjectives follow or cluster with the noun in poetic diction.
segments.s377-g67: جهان (the world) + ویژه کردم is a noun + verb phrase where ویژه کردن (here in past 1st person کردم) means ‘to dedicate/set apart’. به دین خدای is a prepositional phrase ‘for/to the religion of God’ with خدای specifying دین (God’s religion). The verb کردم is the main past-tense action: ‘I made/dedicated.’
segments.s377-g68: This clause continues the speaker’s action without repeating a verb: به کشور ‘over/for the country/realm’ followed by the past participle برافگنده ‘raised’ (used here as resultative of the same subject ‘I’). سایه همای is a poetic noun‑noun grouping ‘the shadow/protection of the Homa’ (the genitive relation is made by juxtaposition rather than an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s377-g69: کسی را (kasi rā) sets ‘no one/anyone’ as the topical/affected item with را for emphasis; بنیز is an archaic adverb here functioning like ‘also/indeed’ or an intensifier; از کسی بیم نه literally ‘there was no fear of anyone’. The clause therefore means ‘No one feared anyone.’ Note the negative نه at the end governs the clause.
segments.s377-g70: به گیتی is a locative ‘in the world’; کسی ... نه again yields ‘there was no one’ because of the clause-final negation نه; بی‌زر و سیم is a compound with the prefix بی‑ ‘without’ meaning ‘without gold and silver’ (i.e. penniless). The whole clause: ‘In the world there was no one without gold and silver.’
segments.s377-g71: فروزنده is an epithet/noun ‘brightener’ or ‘that which makes bright’ applied to گیتی (‘the world’) in an appositive phrase; بسان is a simile particle ‘like/as’, linking the phrase to بهشت ‘paradise’. Together they read ‘the world’s brightener, like paradise.’
segments.s377-g72: جهان گشته آباد uses گشته (perfect participle ‘has become/turned’) with آباد (‘prosperous/inhabited’) to state a resultant condition ‘the world has become prosperous.’ The conjunction و links هر جای کشت ‘and every place (was) cultivated’ — کشت is a nominal form ‘cultivation’ used poetically for ‘(was) sown/cultivated,’ with the verb implied.
segments.s377-g49: بفرمود is a past/honorific verb meaning 'he commanded/said' (courtly register). تا here is a subordinator meaning 'that/so that' introducing a subordinate clause (not temporal 'until'). نامور پهلوان is a noun phrase 'the renowned hero/champion' with adjective before noun (adjective naming quality).
segments.s377-g50: همی is an archaic/poetic continuous/modal particle marking ongoing or habitual action (roughly 'would/should/kept on'). گشت is a past tense/preterite of گشتن used here in the sense 'go/roam' (third person). هر سو به گرد جهان is a prepositional adverbial: هر سو 'every direction', به گرد جهان 'around the world' (به + گرد = 'around' + جهان 'world').
segments.s377-g51: به هرجا که is a fixed relative/locative phrase 'to/at whatever place/wherever'. که introduces the subordinate clause. آن شاه بنهاد روی is a verb phrase: بنهاد (past of بنهادن) 'set/put' used here in the idiom 'بنهاد روی' meaning 'turned his face/took his direction'; آن شاه = 'that king'.
segments.s377-g52: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'came/appeared'. پذیره is a noun 'reception/welcoming' functioning as the subject/thing that 'came'. کسی 'someone' is an indefinite noun; پیش اوی places that someone 'before/ in the presence of him' (پیش = 'before/in front of', اوی = 'him/her' with final ī as an enclitic form).
segments.s377-g53: همه کس = 'everybody' (subject). مر او را is an archaic/dialectal dative/benefactive clitic combination meaning 'to/for him' (مر is an old dative form; او را repeats/marks the person). به فرمان شدند: به فرمان = 'by/on his command' and شدند is past plural 'they became/they were' used here to mean 'they were (made) to obey' or simply 'obeyed'.
segments.s377-g54: بدان is a compound locution here meaning 'to that/thereupon' (ب = به 'to/into' + دان used demonstratively), در جهان پاک is a locative phrase 'in the pure/clean world', and پنهان شدند is a past plural verb phrase 'they hid themselves' or 'they were hidden' (پنهان used as adjective/participle with شدند).
segments.s377-g55: چو = 'when' (temporal conjunction). گیتی همه راست شد = 'the world became/all was made right' (گیتی 'world', همه 'all', راست شد 'became straight/right' — شد is past 'became'). بر پدرش: بر here indicates relation/toward or 'for' and ش is the clitic 'his', so 'for his father' or 'over his father'.
segments.s377-g56: گشاد is an adjective/verb meaning 'wide/open/gaping' describing the state of the belt; از میان = 'from the middle' (از 'from', میان 'middle'); باز here means 'open' (poetic usage), زرین 'golden' modifies کمرش (کمر + ش 'his') where کمر means 'girdle/belt' or 'waist' — together 'his golden belt opened wide at the middle.'
segments.s377-g33: Word order is object (گزارش) + adverbial particle (همی) + past verb (کرد) + subject (اسفندیار). همی is an archaic/progressive particle (here marking a simple past/habitual narration), کرد is the past of کردن meaning 'made/did' (here 'made a report'), and the subject follows the verb as is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s377-g34: به فرمان یزدان is an instrumental/prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' (به + فرمان + یزدان). همی again is the archaic narrative/progressive particle attached to the following verb بست, which is past of بستن used here in the sense 'to close, to bring to an end' (بست کار = 'closed/concluded the affair'). کار is the affected noun 'affair/work'.
segments.s377-g35: چو starts a temporal clause 'when'. آگه شدند is a compound meaning 'became aware' (آگه = aware; شدند = became). از here marks the object of awareness ('aware of'), and نکو modifies دین ('good religion'); اوی is the archaic possessive pronoun 'his' attached after the noun.
segments.s377-g36: گرفتند is past tense 'they took/adopted' (transitive). آن is a demonstrative 'that/the', and راه و آیین اوی is a coordinated noun phrase 'his way and custom' with the possessive اوی ('his') referring back to the same person; the full clause means 'they adopted that way and custom of his.'
segments.s377-g37: بتان is the plural subject 'idols'. از سر کوه is a locative/ablative phrase 'from/on the summit of the mountain' (poetic usage for location). می‌سوختند is the imperfective past 'were burning' (می‑ prefix with past plural suffix indicates ongoing or repeated past action).
segments.s377-g38: بجای is a preposition 'in place of / instead of'. بت = 'idol' is the thing replaced, آذر = 'fire' (here the sacred/ceremonial fire) is what they set up, and برافروختند is the past plural 'they kindled/lit' (برافروختن = to kindle). The sequence says they kindled fire in place of the idol.
segments.s377-g39: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; نامه کردند is a common collocation 'wrote letters' or 'sent letters' (نامه as object + کردند 'they made/did' = 'they wrote'). زی شهریار is an archaic prepositional phrase introducing the addressee 'to the king' — زی is an older preposition here meaning 'to/unto' (this sense is not listed in the provided sense set, see note).
segments.s377-g40: که introduces the reported content 'that'. ما دین گرفتیم is past-tense report 'we took/accepted the religion' (گرفتیم = we took/adopted). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' here, and اسفندیار is the source-name 'Esfandiyar.'
segments.s377-g9: This phrase sets the scene: به + استاد = 'to/for the master' and در پیش او = 'in his presence/before him.' Word order is topical/poetic (preposition + noun + locative phrase). او is the third-person pronoun 'him,' and شیرفش is a proper name (here the addressee).
segments.s377-g10: سرافگنده is an adjective/adverbial meaning 'ashamed/abashed.' و simply links the two actions. دست کرده is a light verbal phrase where کرده (perfect/participial form of کردن) combines with دست to mean 'put his hand (on/at)…'. به کش is a prepositional complement indicating the target/action: کش here is the root 'to draw/pull' (i.e. draw a sword), so دست کرده به کش = 'put his hand to (draw) the sword.'
segments.s377-g11: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' شاه جهان is a fixed epithet 'king of the world' (شاه + جهان). روی او را is the direct object phrase 'his face' with the definite object marker را. بدید is the past tense 'saw' — an older/past form with the b‑ prefix common in classical Persian.
segments.s377-g12: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (or 'out of'); ز جان و جهانش = 'from his life and his world' (emphatic possessions). به دل برگزید is a light-verb construction: به دل = 'into the heart' (i.e. 'in his heart') and برگزید is the past tense 'chose/selected.' Together it expresses 'he chose/made him beloved in his heart, preferring him above life and world.'
segments.s377-g13: بدو is the prepositional/clitic phrase 'to him' (i.e. 'to that person'). گفت is the main verb 'said' (past tense); here it introduces direct speech. شاه‌ای یل اسفندیار is the vocative address: شاه‌ای/یل = 'O hero/valiant one' followed by the name اسفندیار.
segments.s377-g14: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ('indeed/always' in classical usage). آرزو is a noun 'desire/longing.' بایدت is the modal 'must/ought' plus the second-person clitic -ت ('for you' = 'you must'); grammatically بایَد with enclitic gives necessity. کارزار is the object/predicate 'battle,' so the clause means 'battle must be your desire' or 'you must desire battle.'
segments.s377-g15: یل تیغ‌زن is a compound epithet: یل 'hero/brave man' + تیغ‌زن 'sword‑striker/swordsman.' گفت is the past-tense 'said' introducing speech. فرمان is the noun 'command/order.' تراست is an archaic/poetic fused expression meaning 'it is yours' (literally تو + را + است → تراست), i.e. 'belongs to you.'
segments.s377-g16: که is a subordinating conjunction 'that/for,' introducing the reason or explanation. تو is the second-person pronoun 'you.' شهریاری is 'sovereignty/royal rule' (predicate), and گیهان 'world' repeats the domain; تراست again 'is yours' (archaic fused form). The clause explains/justifies why the command belongs to him: 'for you are a ruler and the world is yours.'
segments.s377-g17: کی here is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” نامور is an epithet/adjective placed before the noun (here functioning as “the famed/renowned one”); تاج زرینش is a noun phrase with تاج “crown” and زرین (golden) bearing the possessive clitic -ش (his) — i.e. “his golden crown.” داد is the past-tense verb (3rd sg.) “gave”; the overall order is typical SOV with object(s) placed before the verb.
segments.s377-g18: در here means “door/gate” (not the preposition ‘in’); گنجها را is the definite/marked object “the treasuries/treasure-chambers” (را marks definiteness/object). برو برگشاد is an older/poetic verbal phrase: برگشاد is the past verb “opened,” and برو functions adverbially/episodically with it (thereupon/then — in later Persian it can also be an imperative ‘go’, but here it accompanies the action), so together they mean “then opened (the doors).”
segments.s377-g19: همه کار ایران is a nominal phrase ‘all/the whole affair(s) of Iran’ placed before the verb. سپرد is the past-tense verb ‘entrusted/handed over.’ او را is the dative/accusative marked noun phrase ‘to him’ (را marking the person entrusted to), and the short archaic clitic مر appears before او را — an old dative/recipient-like element; the clause therefore means “(he) entrusted all the affairs of Iran to him.” Word order again is object(s)/theme before verb.
segments.s377-g20: که is the subordinating conjunction ‘that/so that.’ او را is again the marked pronoun ‘him.’ بدی is an abstract noun ‘evil/harm’ and پهلوی is a noun ‘side/flank’ used figuratively; دستبرد is a compound noun ‘seizure/theft/encroachment.’ Grammatically the phrase gives the purpose/result clause: ‘so that the harm (of seizure) would not come to his flank’ (i.e. to guard him from robbery/encroachment).
segments.s377-g21: درفشان is a noun/title here (‘the standard/standard-bearer’ or the standard itself) and is the first direct object. بدو is the dative clitic/phrase ‘to him’ and داد is the past verb ‘gave.’ The coordinating و links further objects (گنج و سپاه — ‘treasure and army’), so the clause lists what was given to him.
segments.s377-g22: هنوزت is the temporal adverb هنوز ‘still/yet’ with a clitic -ت (poetic/archaic); نبد is an archaic negative/existential form meaning ‘was not / did not yet exist.’ گفت here functions with its root meaning related to speech (‘said’/‘speech’) and is part of the clause that follows; هنگام گاه is a tautological pair ‘appointed time / moment’ (both words mean ‘time/occasion’). Together the clause reads ‘it was not yet the time to speak’ — a verbless or predicative negative construction using the archaic نبد.
segments.s377-g23: برو گفت is reported speech: برو is the quoted imperative “Go!” and گفت is the verb of saying; together they mean ‘he said “Go!”’. The second coordinated clause پا را به زین اندر آر has پا را ‘the foot’ (object), به زین ‘into the saddle’ (به + noun), اندر the locative/prepositional particle ‘in/into,’ and آر is an archaic imperative of آوردن/آوردن-like verbs meaning ‘put/bring/place’ (here ‘set/place’). Grammatically this is a speech act followed by an immediate action: ‘he said “Go” and put his foot in the saddle.’
segments.s377-g24: همه کشورت را is the definite object ‘your whole country/realm’ (همه ‘entire’ + کشورت ‘your country’ + را object marker). به دین is a prepositional phrase literally ‘to/into the religion’ (here meaning ‘into the faith/according to the faith or law’), اندر is locative ‘in/within,’ and آر again is the imperative ‘bring/put’ (command). The clause is an imperative: ‘bring your whole kingdom into the faith’ (i.e. organize your realm according to the faith/law).
segments.s377-g1: This phrase locates the person in time: کی is used here as a temporal/poetic particle ('when' / 'then') introducing the clause; نامبردار is a descriptive adjective/noun 'the renowned/aforementioned one'; زان روزگار is a prepositional/time phrase 'from that time'. Together they mean 'from that time the renowned (one)...'.
segments.s377-g2: نشست is a past action 'sat/withdrawed/settled' (here 'withdrew' or 'ceased to attend'); از بر is a fixed classical combination meaning 'from the presence of' or 'away from before'; گاه (here in the sense of the court/occasion) and آن شهریار 'that monarch' complete the phrase 'from the presence of that king.' Word order is typical poetic Persian: verb after the temporal/nominal phrase.
segments.s377-g3: گزینان is a plural participial noun 'the chosen/selected (ones)'; لشکرش را is the definite direct object marked by را ('his army'); بار داد is a compound verb 'gave a burden/loaded' used idiomatically here as 'loaded/filled (the army) with...'. The object (لشکرش را) precedes the verb, a normal order in Persian.
segments.s377-g4: This is a coordinated noun phrase giving who filled the ranks: بزرگان 'the great ones/nobles' و 'and' شاهان 'kings'; مهترنژاد is an adjectival compound 'of chief/foremost lineage' modifying (or appositive to) those nobles and kings.
segments.s377-g5: ز پیش اندر آمد is a compact classical expression 'came from the front/advanced'; اندر آمد is the past tense 'came/entered.' گو here is a brief poetic particle attached before the name (used to introduce the actor) and the name اسفندیار identifies who moved forward. Word order places the movement phrase before the subject.
segments.s377-g6: به دست اندرون is an instrumental/locative phrase 'in (his) inner/right hand' (به ... دست = 'with the hand'), where به is the preposition used as 'by/with'; گرزه نامِ وسیله 'mace' and گاوسار is the proper name of that mace. The whole phrase describes what he held as he advanced.
segments.s377-g7: نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed/put (on)'; the following prepositional/locative sequence به سر بر indicates location 'upon the head'; کیانی qualifies کلاه as 'a Kayanian cap.' Classical Persian permits this relatively free order where the verb (نهاده) precedes the full noun phrase describing what was placed and where.
segments.s377-g8: به زیر کلاهش is a locative phrase 'under his hat' (به + زیر = 'beneath'); همی تافت is a poetic imperfective/continuous past ('was shining' — همی marks durative/ongoing action, تافت is past of 'to shine'); ماه 'the moon' is the shining subject, a common metaphor for a radiant face. The sentence places the image after the action for emphasis.
segments.s377-g41: ببستیم is a 1st‑person plural past perfect/preterite (“we bound/tied”); in classical Persian the prefix be- + past stem gives the past action. کشتی is the direct object in the indefinite form (“the ship/boat”). و is the simple coordinating conjunction “and.” بگرفت is a past 3rd‑person form (here rendered in narration as a simple past) meaning “took/ seized” or, in context of sound, “gave/raised” (i.e. took hold of the cry). باژ is a noun “cry/noise” and is the object of بگرفت.
segments.s377-g42: کنونت is the adverb کنون “now” with an emphatic clitic; it sets the temporal frame. نشاید is an impersonal/epistemic present/past construction meaning “it is not fitting/it ought not to be.” ز here is the classical poetic preposition “from.” ما is the agent/ source “us/we.” خاست is a past form of خاستن meaning “arose/rose up.” باژ is the noun “cry,” the semantic patient of خاست.
segments.s377-g43: که introduces a subordinate clause (“that/for/that it is because”), here giving the reason: ما is the subject “we.” راست گشتیم is a compound verb: راست (adj. “right/upright/true”) + گشتیم (1st‑pl. past of گشتن “to become/turn”), so “we became upright/true.” و links the second nominal predicate ایزدپرست, a compound/compound‑noun meaning “God‑worshipper” or “one who worships Izad (God).”
segments.s377-g44: کنون is the adverb “now.” The sequence زند و استا appears to be two items coordinated by و; in this passage they most likely name persons/titles or things to be dispatched (cf. the next sentence where a زندی is sent), so they function as direct objects/targets of فرست. سوی ما is a directional phrase “toward us.” فرست is the (hortative/imperative or 3rd‑person past‑stem used as a jussive) root meaning “send/dispatch.” (Note: the exact identity and grammatical status of زند and استا is uncertain in isolation— here they are treated as the things to be sent.)
segments.s377-g45: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” شه is the subject “the king.” نامه شهریاران is a genitive phrase: نامه “letter/book” modified by شهریاران “of the rulers/kings.” بخواند is a 3rd‑person past tense verb “he read/recited”; in courtly context بخواند can also mean “he caused to be read” or “he read aloud.” Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal marker → subject → object phrase → verb.
segments.s377-g46: نشست here is a past action “he sat (down)/took a seat.” از برگاه is a prepositional phrase giving place/position relative to the royal platform/court (برگاه = throne or raised royal platform); classical usage can render direction or location with از. و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” یاران بخواند: یاران is the object “his companions,” and بخواند (same form as before) here is best read as “he called/summoned” (a common sense of خواندن in administrative/court contexts).
segments.s377-g47: فرستاد is 3rd‑person singular past “he sent/dispatched.” زندی is the direct object and appears to be a denominal or proper label (a Zand‑person/agent); the -ی suffix marks relation or affiliation. به هر کشوری is a directional/prepositional phrase “to every country/realm”: به = “to,” هر = “every,” کشوری = “country/realm” used adjectivally/nounally here.
segments.s377-g48: The second prepositional phrase repeats به هر “to every” and lists targets: نامداری and مهتری. Both are abstract‑derived nouns used here to mean the prominent persons/positions — “the famed/notable one” (نامداری) and “the chief/elder/preeminent one” (مهتری). The coordinating و links the two kinds of addressees; the whole phrase modifies or specifies the destinations/recipients of the sending.
segments.s377-g57: به شادی نشست = 'he sat (down) in joy'; به here gives the manner ('joyfully' / 'with joy'), followed by the noun شادی. نشست is past‑tense third‑person singular of نشستن ('to sit'). از بر تخت is an older prepositional phrase meaning 'upon the throne' (از + بر + noun; بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne'). و گاه = 'and at times' (گاه as adverb 'occasionally').
segments.s377-g58: بیاسود یک چند گه با سپاه = 'he rested/a little while' (poetic/archaic usage); بیاسود here is a past form meaning 'took rest' (third‑person implied). یک چند گه = 'for a short while / a few moments' (یک چند = 'a few', گه = 'a time/occasion'). با سپاه = 'with the army' (با + noun).
segments.s377-g59: برادرش را خواند فرشیدورد = literal order: 'his brother-OBJ را called Farshidvard'. برادرش is 'his brother' (برادر + possessive suffix ش). را marks it as a definite/direct object. خواند is past third‑person of خواندن; here it means 'called'/'named' (i.e., he called/named him Farshidvard). The name follows the verb as the complement.
segments.s377-g60: سپاهی برون کرد مردان مرد = 'he sent forth a host/army'. سپاهی used as the object/phrase 'a host/army'; برون is an adverb 'out/forth' placed before the verb; کرد is past of کردن ('to do/make') used here in the causative/transitive sense 'sent out / caused to go out'. مردان مرد is an emphatic apposition ('men-of-men'), i.e. heroic/valiant men.
segments.s377-g61: بدو داد و دینار دادش بسی = 'he gave to him, and gave him dinars abundantly.' بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb; داد is past 'gave'. و links the clauses. دینار is the object 'dinar(s)'; دادش is داد + enclitic ش 'gave him'. بسی here qualifies the quantity ('much/abundantly')—classical usage meaning 'many/much'.
segments.s377-g62: خراسان بدو داد و کردش گسی = 'he gave Khorasan to him, and made him someone.' خراسان is the thing given (Khorasan); بدو داد repeats 'gave it to him' (prep‑pronoun + داد). و joins the next clause. کردش is کرد + enclitic ش 'he made him' (past); گسی is an indefinite/indefinite‑like term 'someone' / 'a certain person' used here as the complement of 'made' (i.e., appointed him to some role).
segments.s377-g63: چو یک چند گاهی برآمد برین = temporal clause 'when/after a short time he arose/appeared there'. چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. یک چند گاهی is an adverbial phrase 'for a short while / after a few moments' (یک چند = 'a few', گاهی = 'a time/for a while'). برآمد is past third‑person 'he rose/appeared'. بر + ین together form the classical locative/adverbial 'برین' meaning 'to that/above/there' (poetic/archaic).
segments.s377-g64: جهان ویژه گشت از بد و پاک دین = 'the world/realm became set apart / distinguished; [it was] freed from evil and religion made pure.' جهان is the subject 'the world/realm'. ویژه گشت is a light‑verb construction 'became special/dedicated/set apart' (ویژه = 'special/dedicated', گشت = 'became'). از marks the source or separation 'from'; بد = 'evil'; و = 'and'; پاک دین = 'pure religion' (پاک modifies دین). The overall sense is a transformation: the realm was set apart from evil and religion was purified (classical/poetic phrasing, somewhat condensed).

sentences.s377-s13: The sword-wielding Gerdaksh, the king's son, set out and swept across his realm with an army.
sentences.s377-s14: He proceeded to Rome and to India; he crossed the sea and passed through darkness.
sentences.s377-s15: The kings of Rome, India, and Yemen all gave him the name Tahmatan.
sentences.s377-s16: They sought from him a report concerning religion, and they set the religion aright for Marin.
sentences.s377-s37: The world's horsemen were ever holding it while the tillers sowed seed.
sentences.s377-s38: Thus it had been throughout the world: the world had become lost, bad, and distrustful.
sentences.s377-s33: He sent a messenger to his father: “O famed, triumphant king.”
sentences.s377-s34: I have set the world apart for God’s religion; over the realm I have raised the Homa’s protective shadow.
sentences.s377-s35: No one feared anyone; in the world there was no one without gold and silver.
sentences.s377-s36: The brightener of the world, like paradise — the world has become prosperous, and every place was cultivated.
sentences.s377-s25: He ordered that the famed champion should roam in every direction around the world.
sentences.s377-s26: Wherever that king turned his face, a reception came — someone appeared before him.
sentences.s377-s27: Everyone obeyed him at his command; then they hid themselves in that pure world.
sentences.s377-s28: When the whole world was set right, for his father the golden girdle at his waist opened wide.
sentences.s377-s17: Esfandiyar made a report, and by God's command he closed the affair.
sentences.s377-s18: When they became aware of his good religion, they adopted his way and custom.
sentences.s377-s19: Idols burned on the mountain's summit; in place of the idol they kindled the fire.
sentences.s377-s20: They all wrote letters to the king, saying that we have accepted the religion from Esfandiyar.
sentences.s377-s5: Before his master Shirafsh hung his head in shame and put his hand to his sword.
sentences.s377-s6: When the king of the world saw his face, he chose him in his heart, preferring him even to his life and world.
sentences.s377-s7: The king said to him, 'O valiant Esfandiyar, you must desire battle.'
sentences.s377-s8: The sword‑wielding hero said, 'The command is yours, for you are a sovereign and the world belongs to you.'
sentences.s377-s9: When the famed one gave his golden crown, he opened the doors of the treasuries.
sentences.s377-s10: He entrusted to him the entire administration of Iran, so that harm by seizure would not touch him.
sentences.s377-s11: He gave him the standard, and the treasure and the army; it was not yet the appointed moment to speak.
sentences.s377-s12: He said, “Go,” and set his foot in the saddle; bring your whole realm into the faith.
sentences.s377-s1: From that time the renowned one withdrew from the presence of that monarch.
sentences.s377-s2: He filled his chosen army with the great nobles and kings of foremost lineage.
sentences.s377-s3: Esfandiyar advanced from the front, holding in his hand the inner mace Gausar.
sentences.s377-s4: He placed a Kayanian cap upon his head; beneath his hat the moon shone.
sentences.s377-s21: We moored (tied up) the ship and gave a shout; now it is not fitting that the cry should rise from us.
sentences.s377-s22: That we have become upright and worshippers of God; now send Zand and (…/Estâ) toward us.
sentences.s377-s23: When the king read the letter/book of the rulers, he took his seat on the royal platform and summoned his companions.
sentences.s377-s24: He sent a Zandi (a Zand‑agent/messenger) to every country — to every notable man and every chief.
sentences.s377-s29: He sat upon the throne in joy, and at times rested for a while, sometimes with the army.
sentences.s377-s30: He called his brother 'Farshidvard' and sent forth a host of heroic men.
sentences.s377-s31: He gave him (gifts), gave him many dinars; he granted Khorasan to him and made him someone (i.e. appointed him).
sentences.s377-s32: When, after a short while, he rose into that world, the realm was set apart from evil and religion became pure.
